lessons.title: Dictionaries: key-value pairs
lessons.body: A dictionary stores key-value pairs, accessed by key (not by position, like lists). You create one with `{}`, access with `dictionary[key]`, add or update by assigning a value to a key, and use `.get(key, default)` to access without risking an error when the key doesn't exist.
lessons.example:
student = {'name': 'Ana', 'age': 21, 'course': 'Python'}

print(student['name'])     # access by key
student['age'] = 22         # update an existing value
student['city'] = 'Recife'  # add a new key

# .get() doesn't break if the key doesn't exist
print(student.get('phone', 'not informed'))

# .items() goes through key and value pairs
for key, value in student.items():
    print(f'{key}: {value}')

lessons.title: Tuples: immutable collections
lessons.body: Tuples store several values in order, like lists — but once created they cannot be changed. They're useful when the data shouldn't change (a coordinate, a fixed pair of values). You create one with `()` and can unpack the values into variables at once.
lessons.example:
point = (10, 20)
print(point[0])   # access by index, like a list

# Unpacking: each value goes to a variable
x, y = point
print(f'x={x}, y={y}')

# Trying to change a tuple item gives an error:
# point[0] = 5  -> TypeError

name, age = 'Ana', 21   # a tuple without parentheses also works
print(name, age)

lessons.title: Sets: collections without duplicates
lessons.body: A set stores unique items, with no defined order and no duplicates — adding the same value twice has no effect. It's perfect for removing duplicates from a list or checking if an item exists quickly. You create one with `set()` or `{}` with values inside.

exercises.title: Person's age
exercises.prompt: Write a function `age(person)` that takes a dictionary with the keys 'name' and 'age' and returns the value of the 'age' key.

exercises.title: Update price
exercises.prompt: Write a function `update_price(product, new_price)` that takes a product dictionary (with the key 'price') and returns the same dictionary with the price updated.
hints: Assign the new value: product['price'] = new_price.
Remember to return the dictionary at the end.

exercises.title: Look up with a default value
exercises.prompt: Write a function `phone_or_default(contact)` that returns the phone from the `contact` dictionary, or 'no phone' if the key doesn't exist.

tests.name: returns the phone when it exists
returns the default when it doesn't exist

hints: The .get(key, default) method solves this in one line.

exercises.title: Sum dictionary values
exercises.prompt: Write a function `total_sales(sales)` that takes a dictionary {product: quantity_sold} and returns the sum of all quantities.
hints: The .values() method gives access to just the dictionary's values.

exercises.title: Reverse coordinates
exercises.prompt: Write a function `reverse_point(point)` that takes a tuple (x, y) and returns a new tuple (y, x).
hints: Unpack with x, y = point and return (y, x).

exercises.title: Distance between points
exercises.prompt: Write a function `x_distance(point_a, point_b)` that takes two tuples (x, y) and returns the absolute difference between the x coordinates.
hints: abs(a - b) gives the absolute difference.
Take the first item of each tuple: point_a[0].

exercises.title: Remove duplicates with a set
exercises.prompt: Write a function `unique_items(items)` that returns how many different items (without duplicates) exist in the list.
hints: len(...) counts how many items are left.

exercises.title: Items in common
exercises.prompt: Write a function `in_common(list_a, list_b)` that returns a set with the items that appear in both lists.
hints: Turn the lists into sets with set(list).
The & operator computes the intersection of two sets.
